title: Prabowo Subianto's Risky Indonesian Presidency
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 16 May 2026
content:
Indonesia's President Prabowo Subianto recently took office with grand ambitions, but his current trajectory threatens to unravel decades of national progress. Having spent a quarter of a century clawing his way back to political prominence, he now leads the world's largest Muslim-majority country. However, instead of prioritizing stability, Prabowo has centralized power and surrounded himself with a flock of obedient flatterers. He recently dismissed a highly respected finance minister, replacing her with an official who publicly dismisses global economic realities and crucial fiscal warnings. Consequently, fearful business leaders and intimidated civil society groups are increasingly hesitant to speak out against his risky policies.

The president's current economic strategy relies heavily on unaffordable pet projects that threaten to trigger a devastating fiscal crisis. Funding massive initiatives like free school meals and expansive village co-operatives could easily force the government to breach its legal budget deficit limit. Furthermore, as he makes the economy more precarious, Prabowo is simultaneously eroding the foundational pillars of Indonesian democracy by squelching legislative opposition. A sprawling, multi-ethnic archipelago cannot be successfully governed simply by issuing rigid commands like a military general. To secure his legacy and prevent future unrest, Prabowo must urgently start listening to unpalatable truths rather than surrounding himself with yes-men.
